Council members,

The proposition asks a narrow, verifiable question: before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027, did Polymarket launch an official fungible token and publicly communicate that launch through its own channels, excluding NFTs, promotional giveaways, tokenized points, or tokens primarily launched in collaboration with other projects or communities? Our theory is yes. Under a preponderance standard, it is enough to establish that it is more likely than not that Polymarket itself introduced a transferable, fungible cryptoasset and told the public so through its official outlets before the cutoff.

The real dispute is not about market rumors or community tokens bearing Polymarket’s name. It turns on what counts as (1) “official,” (2) a “fungible token,” and (3) “launched,” and whether reliable, time-stamped evidence ties those elements to Polymarket’s own communications and on-chain actions before the deadline.

Working definitions to guide the record
- Official: A release attributable to Polymarket’s corporate entity or core team, communicated through official channels the company controls—e.g., the primary website, blog, documentation, primary social media accounts, verified announcements in an official community server, or an in-app notice—such that a reasonable user would understand Polymarket to be the issuer.
- Fungible token: A transferable, fungible cryptoasset implementing a standard such as ERC‑20 (or a substantially similar standard on a supported L1/L2), not an NFT, not a non-transferable point, and not merely a stablecoin or wrapped asset used for settlement.
- Launched: A public release event—deployment and/or activation—paired with official public communication that identifies the asset as Polymarket’s token. Mere private testing or an undisclosed contract is not a launch. Conversely, a public announcement without an actually deployable token also fails.
- Exclusions: (a) NFTs, (b) promotional coupons or rewards, (c) tokenized point systems even if transferable later, and (d) tokens whose primary issuer is another project/community with Polymarket only promoting or integrating them.

Factual questions that will decide the case
1) Did Polymarket publish an official, time-stamped communication before the cutoff clearly identifying a fungible token as its own (by name/symbol and, ideally, contract address or chain)?
2) Does on-chain evidence show a fungible token contract deployed and made available to the public, with credible linkage to Polymarket (e.g., known org-controlled deployer, verified contract metadata, or contemporaneous official post that uniquely identifies the contract)?
3) Was the token broadly transferable independent of the Polymarket app (i.e., users could hold and transfer it in standard wallets)?
4) Was the communication “official,” as defined above, rather than third-party press, influencers, community forums, or partner projects speaking on their own?
5) Is the timing unequivocally before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027 (converting on-chain UTC timestamps and platform posts to PST)?
6) Do any exclusions apply (NFT, points, promotional item, or partner/community token) such that—even if discussed by Polymarket—the asset does not qualify?

Proof that will satisfy the burden
- A chain explorer record of a fungible token contract and initial distribution events prior to the cutoff, paired with an official Polymarket communication that identifies this token as its own. The communication should be specific enough (name/symbol/contract/chain) to uniquely tie to the on-chain asset. Evidence of control (e.g., organization-associated deployer or multisig) further strengthens attribution.
- Archived copies of Polymarket-controlled web pages, blog posts, docs, or app banners contemporaneous with the launch and showing the token’s official status.
- Official social media posts or verified announcements from Polymarket that either link the contract or unmistakably identify the token in a way that maps to a unique on-chain asset.
- Exchange or listing announcements only to the extent they reference and are corroborated by an official Polymarket statement; third-party claims alone are insufficient.

Proof that would defeat the burden
- Absence of any official Polymarket communication identifying a fungible token as its own before the cutoff.
- Evidence that any referenced asset was an NFT, a non-transferable or custodial point/reward, or otherwise not a standard fungible token.
- Evidence that the token was principally issued by a partner/community, with Polymarket acting only as an integrator or promoter.
- Evidence that timing misses the deadline once normalized to PST, or that any relevant announcement post-dates the cutoff.

Anticipated areas of dispute and how to resolve them
- “Official channel” ambiguity: prioritize content from Polymarket-controlled properties and verified accounts over press or community chatter. If ambiguity remains, corroborate across multiple Polymarket-controlled sources.
- “Launch” versus “announcement”: require both on-chain availability and public identification by Polymarket. Either element alone is not enough.
- “Tokenized points” or rebrands: a later conversion of points into a token after the cutoff does not retroactively satisfy the proposition; conversely, a properly launched fungible token before the cutoff suffices regardless of any future rebrand.
- Collaborations: if the asset is co-marketed but issuer-of-record is another project/DAO, it is excluded unless Polymarket’s own materials state that it is Polymarket’s token and the on-chain facts align.

Forward-looking plan for the record
- Collect and archive Polymarket’s official announcements, web pages, documentation, and in-app notices bearing clear timestamps.
- Identify the candidate token(s) and obtain chain explorer records (contract verification, deployment, initial mint/transfer, ownership) with timestamps converted to PST.
- Link official communications to the unique on-chain asset via name/symbol/contract/chain and, where possible, org-controlled deployers or verified metadata.
- Compile any contrary official statements (e.g., "no token") to squarely address and, if appropriate, distinguish them by date, scope, or context.

Under the preponderance standard, we do not need unanimity across every artifact; we need the more credible, better-aligned set of official communications and on-chain facts to outweigh alternatives. That is the showing we aim to make: that Polymarket itself launched and publicly identified a fungible token before the deadline, satisfying the proposition’s elements and surviving the exclusions.

What is actually disputed
- Whether “official,” “fungible token,” and “launched” are met by Polymarket’s own actions and communications, not third‑party chatter.
- Whether any candidate asset is excluded (NFT, promo, points, or primarily another project’s token) even if Polymarket mentioned it.
- Whether reliable timestamps place both on‑chain availability and official communication before the cutoff when normalized to PST.

Guiding definitions for the record
- Official: Communication on channels Polymarket controls (primary website, blog/docs, verified social accounts, official community announcements, in‑app notices) that would lead a reasonable user to attribute issuance to Polymarket.
- Fungible token: A transferable cryptoasset implementing a standard like ERC‑20 (or analogous L1/L2 standard), not an NFT or non‑transferable points, and not merely a generic settlement asset outside Polymarket’s issuance.
- Launched: Publicly released and identified by Polymarket as its token. Announcement without a deployed token, or a silent contract without official identification, does not suffice.
- Exclusions: NFTs, promotions/coupons, tokenized points (even if later made transferable), and tokens primarily issued by partners/communities with Polymarket only integrating or co‑marketing them.

Factual questions that will decide the case

2) Is there on‑chain evidence of a fungible token contract made publicly available before the cutoff, with credible linkage to Polymarket (e.g., known deployer, verified metadata, or contemporaneous official identification)?
3) Was the token broadly transferable in standard wallets, independent of the app?
4) Do the communications originate from Polymarket channels rather than press, influencers, or partners?
5) Do timestamps (posts and on‑chain) converted to PST predate the deadline?
6) Do any exclusions apply notwithstanding Polymarket mentions?

- Archived Polymarket web pages, blog/docs, or in‑app notices with contemporaneous timestamps identifying the token as official.
- Official social posts or verified community announcements that either link the contract or identify the token in a way that maps uniquely to the on‑chain asset.

Proof that would defeat the burden
- No official Polymarket identification of a fungible token before the cutoff.
- The referenced asset is an NFT, non‑transferable/custodial points, or otherwise not a standard fungible token.
- The primary issuer is a partner/community, with Polymarket only integrating or promoting it.
- Timestamps, once normalized to PST, fall after the deadline.

Anticipated disputes and resolution
- “Official channel” ambiguity: favor Polymarket‑controlled properties and verified accounts; require corroboration if ambiguous.
- “Launch” vs “announcement”: require both on‑chain availability and official identification; either alone is insufficient.
- Points‑to‑token conversions or rebrands: post‑cutoff conversions do not retroactively meet the proposition; a pre‑cutoff fungible token with proper identification does.
- Collaborations: co‑marketing is not enough unless Polymarket’s own materials state it is Polymarket’s token and the on‑chain facts align.

Forward‑looking plan
- Collect and archive Polymarket’s official announcements, web pages, docs, and in‑app notices with clear timestamps.
- Identify candidate token(s); gather explorer records (deployment, minting, ownership) and convert timestamps to PST.
- Link official communications to the unique on‑chain asset via name/symbol/contract/chain and, where possible, org‑controlled deployers or verified metadata.
- Surface and address contrary official statements (e.g., disclaimers of any token) by date and scope, to reconcile or distinguish them.

Under the preponderance standard, our task is to assemble the better‑aligned set of Polymarket‑controlled communications and on‑chain facts showing that, more likely than not, Polymarket launched and publicly identified a fungible token before the deadline, while the exclusions do not apply.

Council members,

The proposition asks whether, before 00:00 PST on January 1, 2027, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels, with clear exclusions for NFTs, promos, point systems, and partner/community tokens. Ambiguity, third‑party speculation, integrations, or generic settlement assets are insufficient.

Where the real dispute lies
- “Official” must mean primary issuance and control by Polymarket (e.g., deployer/owner is Polymarket‑controlled), plus explicit identification on Polymarket‑owned channels—not merely language that could “lead a reasonable user” to infer attribution.
- “Launched” must be a public release of a live, transferable fungible token contract, not just an announcement, roadmap, points program, or a partner/community token that Polymarket integrates or mentions.
- “Fungible token” means a transferable token implementing a standard (e.g., ERC‑20 analogs), usable in standard wallets; custodial balances or in‑app points do not qualify.
- “Official channels” excludes press, third‑party forums, partner posts, retweets without independent endorsement, or unverified community spaces; it must be Polymarket‑controlled properties.
- Time normalization: both the on‑chain record and the official communication must predate the cutoff when converted to PST; either one missing or late defeats the proposition.

Factual questions that will decide the case
1) Did a Polymarket‑controlled channel, within the period, explicitly state that a particular fungible token is Polymarket’s token (by name/symbol and, ideally, contract/chain)?
2) Is there a publicly verifiable fungible token contract available before the cutoff, with credible technical attribution to Polymarket (known deployer, ownership, or verified metadata)?
3) Does the token meet transferability standards in normal wallets, independent of the Polymarket app?
4) Are any candidate assets excluded (NFTs, promos, points, IOUs, wrapped/mirrored assets, or tokens primarily issued by partners/communities)?
5) Are the official communications independently archived and timestamped; do those timestamps, and the block time of the token contract, both fall before the deadline in PST?
6) Do any Polymarket communications disclaim having a token during the period; if so, how are those reconciled?
7) Is there a unique, unambiguous mapping from the official communication to a specific on‑chain token (no name collisions or multiple plausible contracts)?

Proof that would satisfy the burden (and what it must contain)
- A Polymarket‑controlled, time‑stamped communication within the window that clearly identifies the token as Polymarket’s own and uniquely links to the on‑chain asset (by contract address/chain or an identification that can only refer to one contract).
- On‑chain explorer evidence showing the token contract was live before the cutoff, with deployer/owner or verifications credibly tied to Polymarket (e.g., organization‑controlled multisig, verified metadata).
- Evidence of real transferability in standard wallets. Archival records that preserve the content and timestamps. Consistency across all Polymarket official channels.

Proof that would defeat the burden
- No explicit, time‑stamped Polymarket communication identifying a fungible token as its own before the deadline.
- Evidence that the asset is an NFT, non‑transferable points/custodial balance, promotional coupon, wrapped/mirrored IOU, or a partner/community token where Polymarket is not the issuer.
- Ambiguous or conflicting communications; inability to uniquely link any official statement to a specific contract; timestamps that, once normalized to PST, fall after the cutoff; or credible contrary statements from Polymarket within the period.

Anticipated disputes and how to resolve them
- Ambiguity in “official”: require explicit language of ownership/issuance and Polymarket‑controlled publishing.
- “Announcement vs. launch”: require both a live token contract and official identification within the window; one without the other is insufficient.
- Name collisions and cross‑chain variants: demand a unique mapping to one contract and a credible issuer link.
- Points‑to‑token narratives: later conversions or rebrands cannot satisfy a pre‑cutoff token launch.

Forward‑looking plan to build the record
- Collect and archive all Polymarket‑controlled web pages, blogs/docs, verified social posts, and in‑app notices within the period with immutable timestamps, and normalize all times to PST.
- Identify any candidate token(s) across relevant chains; pull explorer records (deployment, ownership, minting), and test transferability in standard wallets.
- Establish organizational attribution: known deployers, multisig signers, verified metadata, or other cryptographic links tying the contract to Polymarket.
- Apply an exclusions checklist (NFT/promo/points/partner issuance/wrapped assets) to each candidate.
- Surface any contrary official statements and place them in a dated sequence.

Under a preponderance standard, uncertainty or ambiguity weighs against the proposition. Unless the plaintiff produces clear, time‑aligned, and uniquely linked official communications plus on‑chain issuance by Polymarket itself, the Council should find the proposition not proven.

Official-source record after the 2026-05-03 open-record arbitration.

The run relied on official Polymarket documentation and Polygonscan corroboration discovered by the search-enabled attorney. After the run, the main cited pages were captured under source-captures/.

Captured official Polymarket pages:
- source-captures/polymarket-docs-pusd.html
  URL: https://docs.polymarket.com/concepts/pusd
  Key text from fetched markdown: "pUSD (Polymarket USD) is the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket. It's a standard ERC-20 token on Polygon, backed by USDC." The page states pUSD is transferable, has 6 decimals, is backed by USDC, and links to the Contracts page.
- source-captures/polymarket-docs-contracts.html
  URL: https://docs.polymarket.com/resources/contracts
  Key text from fetched markdown: "All Polymarket contracts are deployed on Polygon mainnet (Chain ID: 137). This is the single source of truth for all contract addresses used across the platform." Under Collateral Contracts, the page lists "pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)" at 0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB.
- source-captures/polymarket-docs-changelog.html
  URL: https://docs.polymarket.com/changelog
  Key text from fetched markdown: Apr 17, 2026 entry says Polymarket is shipping "new Exchange contracts, a rewritten CLOB backend, and a new collateral token (pUSD)" and that exchange upgrades go live April 28, 2026 at ~11:00 UTC. Apr 28, 2026 entry says "CLOB V2 is live on production" and points to the migration guide for pUSD.
- source-captures/polymarket-docs-v2-migration.html
  URL: https://docs.polymarket.com/v2-migration
  Key text from fetched markdown: "Polymarket has shipped a coordinated upgrade of its entire trading infrastructure: new Exchange contracts, a rewritten CLOB backend, and a new collateral token (Polymarket USD, or pUSD)." It says CLOB V2 is live as of April 28, 2026 and identifies pUSD as standard ERC-20 collateral backed by USDC.

Captured corroborating explorer page:
- source-captures/polygonscan-pusd-token.html
  URL: https://polygonscan.com/address/0xC011a7E12a19f7B1f670d46F03B03f3342E82DFB
  Fetched page title identifies "Polymarket: pUSD Token" at the same address. The extracted page shows current transactions, a contract creation row, and Solidity source metadata for CollateralToken. Explorer evidence is corroborative. The official Polymarket Contracts page is the stronger attribution evidence.

Capture manifest:
- source-captures/capture-manifest.txt

- These captures were made after the arbitration, not before attorney filings.
- The captured HTML files should be treated as post-run preservation of discovered evidence, not as evidence available in the original thin packet.
- Server publication timestamps and archive snapshots remain worth adding before any closed-record run.

technical_reports.title: PST normalization and token‑classification analysis

payload.text:
Council members,

Issue and standard
The proposition asks whether, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, excluding NFTs, promos, point systems, and collaborations. Our burden is preponderance: more likely than not.

Bottom line
The record shows Polymarket launched pUSD (Polymarket USD) as a standard ERC‑20 collateral token on Polygon in April 2026 and announced it on Polymarket‑controlled documentation pages, including a dated changelog and migration guide. Polymarket’s Contracts page identifies the unique on‑chain pUSD contract address. This satisfies every element and none of the exclusions. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

Element‑by‑element application
1) Official identification on Polymarket channels
— Polymarket’s own pUSD concept page states that “pUSD (Polymarket USD) is the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” identifies it as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, and provides functional details (transferability, 6 decimals, USDC‑backed). [PX‑1; summarized in PX‑2]
— The Polymarket Contracts page is described as the “single source of truth” for addresses and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with its production address on Polygon mainnet (chain ID 137). [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— The Polymarket docs changelog and V2 migration guide, both official documentation pages, announce the introduction of a “new collateral token (pUSD)” and then report the production go‑live of the new exchange plus pUSD. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
These are Polymarket‑controlled channels squarely within the definition of “official.”

2) On‑chain availability and unique mapping
— The Contracts page uniquely maps Polymarket’s named token “pUSD” to a single Polygon address (0xC011…2E82DFB) and identifies it as the collateral token proxy. [PX‑2]
— The record includes a Polygonscan capture of that address labeled “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” corroborating that this is a live ERC‑20 with transfers. While explorer labeling is corroborative only, the authoritative linkage is Polymarket’s own Contracts page. [PX‑2]

3) Fungibility and transferability
— pUSD is documented as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon with 6 decimals. ERC‑20 tokens are transferable and fungible in standard wallets. The record’s concept page and explorer capture show exactly that. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

4) Public communication prior to the deadline (time normalization to PST)
— Changelog entry dated April 17, 2026 announces the upcoming launch of pUSD alongside exchange upgrades with target go‑live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC. April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC equals April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT (UTC‑7). The same day’s entry reports production go‑live and directs users to the migration guide. Both dates are months before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST cutoff. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

5) Exclusions checklist
— Not an NFT: pUSD is an ERC‑20 fungible token. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— Not a promo or points: It is the platform’s collateral token for all trading, not a coupon or non‑transferable credit. [PX‑1]
— Not a partner/community token: The issuer attribution is Polymarket’s own contracts and documentation; “backed by USDC” does not convert issuance into a collaboration launch by another project. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

Addressing anticipated defenses
— “Collateral token ≠ official token.” The proposition requires an “official fungible token” launched and publicly communicated by Polymarket; it does not require governance or revenue‑sharing features. pUSD is an officially introduced, Polymarket‑branded, fungible ERC‑20 deployed for platform use and identified by Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” Contracts page. That squarely fits the ordinary meaning of “official fungible token.” [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— “Announcement vs. launch.” The record shows both: pre‑announcement (Apr 17, 2026) and production go‑live (Apr 28, 2026), with on‑chain address concurrently identified in Polymarket’s Contracts page. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— “Prior disclaimers of any token.” The record contains no contrary Polymarket disclaimer within the period that would negate pUSD. Generic statements in other contexts typically refer to governance/airdrop tokens; even if present, they would not rebut the specific, later official launch of pUSD documented here. (No such contrary exhibit appears in this packet.)

What the record shows vs. our inferences
— Record facts: Polymarket’s pUSD concept page, Contracts page (address mapping), changelog entries with April 2026 dates, V2 migration guide, and a Polygonscan page for the same address. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— Inference: From “ERC‑20 with 6 decimals on Polygon” and explorer evidence of transfers, we infer fungibility and wallet‑level transferability—consistent with the ERC‑20 standard and the platform role described in the docs.

Disposition
Under a preponderance standard, the better‑aligned, source‑controlled evidence shows Polymarket launched and publicly identified a fungible token—pUSD—well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. The exclusions do not apply. The proposition is therefore more likely than not true. The Council should find for the plaintiff and answer YES.

Exhibits offered
— PX‑1: official-source-record.txt (catalog of captured Polymarket official pages and explorer corroboration)
— PX‑2: primary-evidence.txt (material facts extracted from each captured official page, including the pUSD address and dated changelog entries)
— PX‑3: market-page.txt (verbatim resolution language and exclusions for the question at issue)

Technical report offered
— TR‑1 (PST normalization and token‑classification analysis): Confirms April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT and explains why a Polymarket‑documented ERC‑20 collateral token satisfies “official fungible token” under the resolution’s plain language and exclusions.

technical_reports.summary: Analyzes the ordinary‑meaning distinction between a public token launch and introduction of an internal collateral ERC‑20; explains why editable docs with no immutable timestamps or on‑chain deployer/owner linkage do not, by themselves, establish issuance or timely public communication under a preponderance standard. No outside sources used; analysis is confined to PX‑1/PX‑2 and the market text.
technical_reports.title: Interpretation of “official token launch” and evidentiary weight on this record

payload.text:
Council members,

Issue and burden
The proposition requires proof that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched a fungible token, (ii) officially released it, and (iii) publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels. The standard is preponderance: the plaintiff must tip the scales, not merely suggest a possibility.

What the record shows (and what it does not)
— The record’s only evidence comes through plaintiff‑authored summaries PX‑1 and PX‑2. Those summaries quote or paraphrase Polymarket docs pages that describe “a new collateral token (pUSD),” list a Contracts page entry (“pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with a Polygon address), and include a Changelog with entries dated April 17, 2026 (pre‑announcement) and April 28, 2026 (go‑live ~11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT). PX‑2 also cites a Polygonscan page labeled “Polymarket: pUSD Token.”
— Critically, the underlying captured HTML pages are not in the visible record; only after‑the‑fact narrative extracts are. PX‑1 itself concedes the captures were created “after the arbitration,” that server‑dated headers and immutable archives were not preserved, and that timestamps/publication histories remain “worth adding.” No on‑chain block‑time evidence, deployer/owner identity, or cryptographic attribution to Polymarket appears in the exhibits.

Why this does not meet the proposition under preponderance
1) “Launched an official fungible token” vs. internal collateral plumbing. The only Polymarket phrasing the record attributes to official channels is “collateral token (pUSD).” That describes an internal settlement instrument used to operate the exchange, not a public token launch as an independent product. The proposition’s ordinary reading contemplates that Polymarket launched a token as such; the record shows an infrastructure upgrade that happens to implement an ERC‑20 wrapper for USDC. Absent explicit self‑identification as “our official token” or a comparable statement of issuance/launch, the plaintiff’s theory relies on inference, not text.
2) Issuer attribution is unproven. The Contracts page summary is an editable documentation page. The record supplies no immutable archive, no deployer/owner address tying the contract to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig, and no cryptographic link. Polygonscan labeling is expressly “corroborative only” even per PX‑1. On this record, issuance by Polymarket is alleged, not established.
3) Timing is not immutably established on official channels. The dates cited (April 17 and April 28, 2026) appear only as text in a changelog summarized by PX‑1/PX‑2. There are no server headers, archive snapshots, or signed social posts in evidence. Under preponderance, contemporaneous, immutable timestamps matter; the plaintiff chose not to provide them.
4) The structure of the exclusions reinforces the ordinary‑meaning distinction. Excluding NFTs, promos, and point systems signals the market’s focus on a token “launched by Polymarket” in the way that crypto users commonly understand—a token product held/used beyond mere internal accounting. By contrast, the record’s “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” is a USDC‑backed wrapper minted/burned to serve exchange collateralization. Treating any ERC‑20 used internally as an “official token launch” would collapse the resolution’s distinction and sweep in routine contract refactors.

Fallback even under plaintiff’s broader theory
If the Council were inclined to treat any ERC‑20 named in Polymarket docs as an “official token,” the record still fails to uniquely and sufficiently prove: (a) deployer/owner linkage to Polymarket, (b) immutable, pre‑deadline publication of the claimed announcements, and (c) wallet‑level transferability in practice rather than assertion. The plaintiff asks you to bridge each of these with inference.

Record/argument separation
— Record facts: PX‑1/PX‑2 report that Polymarket docs used the words “collateral token (pUSD),” list a contracts address, and show dated changelog entries in April 2026. PX‑1 admits the captures are post‑run preservations and that archival/timestamp evidence is missing.
— Our inferences: From that thin record, it is at least equally plausible that pUSD is an internal wrapper token whose introduction was a technical migration, not an “official token launch” as contemplated by the proposition.

Disposition
On this record, ambiguity persists on the core element—whether Polymarket “launched an official fungible token.” The plaintiff’s evidence depends on editable documentation, lacks immutable timestamps and issuer attribution, and frames a collateral‑contract migration as a token launch. Under a preponderance standard, that does not tip the scales. The Council should find the proposition not proven and answer NO.

Dates for clarity
— Claimed announcements: April 17, 2026; go‑live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC (April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT).
— Deadline: January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST.

We do not rely on any material outside the visible record; we flag the record’s gaps below for weight and credibility.

payload.text:
Council members,

The defense’s objections do not defeat the proposition under a preponderance standard. They mainly demand evidentiary formalities (immutable archives, deployer-key proof) that the market language never requires. The question asks whether, before Jan 1, 2027 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through official Polymarket channels—excluding NFTs, promos, points, and collaborations. The record satisfies each element.

1) “Collateral token ≠ official token” misreads the rule
— The market does not restrict “official token” to governance/airdrop/revenue tokens. It explicitly lists what does not count (NFTs, promos, points, collaborations) and says nothing about excluding a platform’s collateral token. By expressio unius, reading in a new exclusion would rewrite the market. pUSD is a standard ERC‑20, branded Polymarket USD, used for all trading. That is as “official” as a platform token gets. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

2) Official Polymarket communications are clear and explicit
— Polymarket’s pUSD page: “pUSD (Polymarket USD) is the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket… a standard ERC‑20 token on Polygon,” transferable, 6 decimals, USDC‑backed, and it links to the Contracts page. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— Contracts page (self‑described “single source of truth”) identifies “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with a unique Polygon mainnet address. That is Polymarket itself mapping a named token to a contract. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— Changelog (Apr 17, 2026) announces “a new collateral token (pUSD)” with go‑live target Apr 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC; Apr 28 entry states V2 is live and directs users to the migration guide. That is a public, time‑stamped communication by Polymarket. Converted to local time, Apr 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = Apr 28, 2026 04:00 PDT—well before the Jan 1, 2027 deadline. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

3) Issuer attribution is sufficiently established for this standard
— The proposition requires “officially released by Polymarket,” not cryptographic proof of deployer keys. Polymarket’s own docs name, describe, and uniquely address‑map pUSD as the platform’s collateral token. That is an organizational attestation of issuance/release. Explorer labeling is only corroboration, and we do not rely on it for attribution. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

4) Fungibility/transferability are met; exclusions don’t apply
— The docs call pUSD a standard ERC‑20 with 6 decimals and note transferability. ERC‑20 is by definition fungible in standard wallets. This is not an NFT, not a promotional coupon or points system, and not primarily a partner/community token; “backed by USDC” does not convert Polymarket’s own token into a collaboration launch. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

5) Timing suffices under preponderance
— The record contains dated Polymarket pages announcing and then reporting go‑live on Apr 28, 2026 and mapping the token to a mainnet address. The defense offers no contrary dates or Polymarket statements. Under preponderance, those official communications are enough to find that launch and announcement occurred pre‑deadline. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

6) Record‑integrity critique goes to weight, not outcome
— Yes, the copies were preserved after the open‑record run. But both sides cite the same Polymarket pages and there is no competing exhibit suggesting those pages said anything else. On this record, it is more likely than not that the pages accurately reflect Polymarket’s contemporaneous communications.

Disposition
Polymarket publicly introduced and identified a fungible ERC‑20 token—Polymarket USD (pUSD)—and mapped it to a unique on‑chain address on its own “single source of truth” Contracts page, with go‑live on Apr 28, 2026 (Apr 28, 2026 04:00 PDT). None of the exclusions apply. The scale tips for YES. The proposition is proven by a preponderance of the evidence.

Council members,

Plaintiff’s rebuttal turns on two moves: (1) redefining “official token launch” to mean “any ERC‑20 Polymarket uses,” and (2) elevating editable docs to dispositive proof of both “official release” and “public communication of a launch.” On this record, neither move tips the scale under a preponderance standard.

1) Collateral ≠ “official token launch” on this record
• The proposition’s elements are affirmative: Polymarket must have launched a token and publicly communicated that launch. The exclusions only say what does not count; they do not transform every non‑excluded instrument into a launch. 
• PX‑1/PX‑2 consistently describe pUSD as “the collateral token” introduced within a coordinated infrastructure upgrade (CLOB V2). That is internal plumbing, not a standalone token product launched to holders as “the Polymarket token.” The cited materials never quote Polymarket saying “we launched a token” or “this is our official token.” They say the exchange now uses a new collateral token.

2) “Officially released by Polymarket” is not established by an address listing
• The “Contracts” page mapping “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” to a Polygon address shows integration and nomenclature. It is not proof of issuance or a token launch. The record has no deployer/owner linkage, no statement of supply control, and no communication that Polymarket is issuing a token to the public. 
• The same pages say pUSD is “backed by USDC,” reinforcing that it functions as a wrapped/settlement instrument for trading, not a launched token in the ordinary sense contemplated by the proposition.

3) “Publicly communicated launch” is missing
• The changelog entries (Apr 17, 2026 pre‑announcement; Apr 28, 2026 go‑live ≈ Apr 28, 2026 04:00 PDT) describe a platform upgrade that includes “a new collateral token (pUSD).” That is a component note in a technical release log, not a public launch announcement of a token. The proposition requires Polymarket to have publicly communicated the token’s launch, not merely documented an internal contract swap after shipping.

4) Timing does not cure the categorical gap
• Even accepting plaintiff’s dates and PST normalization, the question is not when an upgrade happened but whether Polymarket launched a token and said so. On this record, the most that is shown is an infrastructure migration that uses an ERC‑20 as collateral. That is equally—if not more—consistent with “no token launch” under the proposition’s ordinary meaning.

5) This is about weight, not hyper‑formal proof
• We are not demanding cryptographic signatures. We are asking for clear language from Polymarket that the thing purportedly “launched” is a token launch at all. The only Polymarket words in evidence—“collateral token,” “single source of truth” for addresses, “CLOB V2 is live”—support the infrastructure reading as much as the plaintiff’s.

Bottom line
• Accepted record facts: pUSD is described in Polymarket docs as a standard ERC‑20 used as collateral; a contracts page lists its address; a changelog on Apr 17/28, 2026 references the upgrade. 
• Missing for the proposition: an explicit Polymarket statement that it “launched” a token as such or that pUSD is Polymarket’s “official token,” plus credible issuer linkage beyond an address label in docs.

If two readings are in equipoise, the moving party has not carried the burden. On this record, “official token launch” remains at least equally plausibly unproven. The Council should answer NO.

Council members,

Question and burden
You are deciding whether, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, with NFTs, promos, points, and partner/community tokens excluded. Our burden is preponderance—more likely than not.

What the record actually establishes
— Identification on official channels: Polymarket’s own documentation describes “Polymarket USD (pUSD)” as “the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, transferable, 6 decimals, USDC‑backed, and links to the Contracts page. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— Unique contract mapping by Polymarket: The official “Contracts” page—self‑described as the “single source of truth” for Polymarket addresses—lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” and gives a single Polygon mainnet address for it (chain ID 137). That is Polymarket itself mapping the named token to a concrete on‑chain contract. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— Public communication and timing: The Polymarket docs changelog records (i) April 17, 2026: introduction of “a new collateral token (pUSD)” as part of the upgrade, with go‑live targeted for April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC, and (ii) April 28, 2026: “CLOB V2 is live on production” with a pointer to the migration guide discussing pUSD. April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT—months before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— On‑chain corroboration: A Polygonscan capture shows the same address labeled for pUSD with live ERC‑20 activity. While corroborative only, it matches the official mapping. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

Element‑by‑element application
1) “Officially released by Polymarket.” Polymarket’s docs do more than mention an internal component—they name the token (Polymarket USD, pUSD), describe what it is, and point to the unique contract on a page Polymarket calls its “single source of truth.” That is an organizational release and identification of a token Polymarket controls and intends users to interact with. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
2) “Fungible token.” The docs characterize pUSD as a standard ERC‑20 with transferability and 6 decimals; ERC‑20 is the archetypal fungible, transferable token. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
3) “Publicly communicated through official Polymarket channels.” The communication occurs on Polymarket‑controlled documentation: the pUSD concept page, the Contracts page, the Changelog, and the V2 migration guide. Those are official channels plainly within the rule. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
4) “Before the deadline.” The relevant communications and go‑live are dated April 17 and April 28, 2026; 11:00 UTC on April 28 converts to 04:00 PDT on April 28, 2026—well before January 1, 2027 00:00 PST. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
5) Exclusions. pUSD is not an NFT, not a promotional offering or points system, and not a token launched in collaboration with another project or community; “backed by USDC” describes collateralization, not primary issuance by a partner. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

Addressing the defense’s strongest points
— “Collateral token ≠ an ‘official token launch.’” The market text does not carve collateral tokens out. It defines what does not count (NFTs, promos, points, collaborations) and otherwise asks whether Polymarket launched an official fungible token and said so. pUSD is Polymarket‑branded, fungible ERC‑20 collateral “used for all trading,” officially mapped to an address by Polymarket itself. That comfortably fits “official fungible token” under the ordinary meaning of the words used. Reading in a new exclusion would rewrite the resolution. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
— “Editable docs and no deployer proof.” Under preponderance, contemporaneous official documentation can carry the day absent contrary evidence. The Contracts page is Polymarket’s declared “single source of truth” and uniquely links pUSD to a contract; the Changelog/migration guide supply dates. The defense offers no counter‑exhibit, no contrary Polymarket statement, and no alternative attribution—only speculation that stronger proofs could exist. That affects weight, not sufficiency. [PX‑1, PX‑2; DX‑1]
— “No explicit words ‘we launched a token.’” The rule requires that Polymarket launched a fungible token and publicly communicated that launch. The changelog’s April 17 announcement of “a new collateral token (pUSD)” and the April 28 go‑live entry, together with the concept and Contracts pages, are the official communication of that launch. The ordinary listener would understand this as Polymarket introducing and turning on its token for use. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

Why the scale tips YES
The plaintiff’s evidence is direct and source‑controlled: Polymarket’s own pages name, describe, and uniquely address‑map pUSD; they announce and confirm go‑live in April 2026. The defense does not present competing facts—only hypotheticals about stronger proof or alternative interpretations. On this record, the better, simpler explanation is that Polymarket launched and publicly communicated its own fungible token, pUSD, before the cutoff.

Disposition
Under the preponderance standard, the proposition is more likely than not true. The Council should find that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token—pUSD—and publicly communicated that launch through its official documentation. Answer: YES.

This record is source‑controlled, specific, and time‑stamped by Polymarket: it names pUSD, states it is an ERC‑20, provides the unique address on a page styled as authoritative, and records pre‑deadline go‑live. The defense supplies no conflicting fact; its critique goes to degree, not direction. Under preponderance, that suffices.

Disposition
The better‑supported reading of the record is that, well before January 1, 2027 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched and publicly communicated an official, fungible ERC‑20 token—Polymarket USD (pUSD). None of the enumerated exclusions apply. The Council should answer YES.

Council members,

Question and burden
You must decide whether, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels. The plaintiff bears the preponderance burden.

What the record actually shows (and no more)
• Polymarket docs describe “a new collateral token (pUSD)” used for all trading, stating it is a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, 6 decimals, transferable, USDC‑backed. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
• The “Contracts” page—styled as a “single source of truth”—lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with a Polygon mainnet address. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
• The Changelog records Apr 17, 2026 (introducing a new collateral token, with a target go‑live) and Apr 28, 2026 (CLOB V2 live; see migration guide). In PST, Apr 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = Apr 28, 2026 04:00 PDT. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
• A Polygonscan page identifies the same address as “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” offered only as corroboration. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
• The copies of these pages were preserved after the run; immutable archives, server‑dated headers, and deployer/owner linkage are not in the packet. [PX‑1; DX‑1]

Decisive points
1) “Launched an official fungible token” vs. introducing collateral plumbing
The only quoted Polymarket phrasing in evidence is “collateral token (pUSD)” shipped as part of a coordinated infrastructure upgrade (CLOB V2). Nothing in the record shows Polymarket characterizing this as a token launch to the public or “our official token.” Treating every ERC‑20 used internally as a “launched official token” stretches the ordinary meaning of “launched” in this market. On this record, pUSD reads as an internal settlement instrument, not a standalone token product launch. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

2) “Officially released by Polymarket” is not established by an address listing
The Contracts page maps names to addresses for many core components. That mapping is evidence of integration, not issuance. There is no deployer/owner attribution, no statement that Polymarket issued a token to the public, and no communication about supply control or distribution—features one would expect if a token were “launched” as such. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

3) “Publicly communicated that launch” is missing
The Changelog documents a platform release that includes a new collateral token. It does not read as a token‑launch announcement. The record contains no Polymarket blog post, in‑app notice, or social post announcing that Polymarket launched a token; only documentation and a technical migration guide mention a collateral component. Under a preponderance standard, equating a component note with a public “token launch” communication is at least equally uncertain. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

4) Timing, while uncontested, does not cure the categorical gap
Assuming Apr 17 and Apr 28, 2026 dates and the PST conversion are accurate, those dates tell us when the upgrade went live—not that Polymarket launched a token or publicly communicated that launch. Timing does not answer the “what” that the proposition requires. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

5) Record weight and integrity
PX‑1 itself flags that captures were made after the run and that server timestamps/archives were not preserved. We do not demand cryptographic proof, but when the core question turns on whether there was a “launch” and “public communication of that launch,” the absence of explicit launch language and immutable publication evidence leaves the scale at least in equipoise. [PX‑1; DX‑1]

Addressing plaintiff’s strongest points
• “Official channel + ERC‑20 + unique address mapping” is not the same as a launch. The proposition is affirmative: Polymarket must have launched a token and publicly communicated that launch. The words in record—“collateral token (pUSD),” “single source of truth,” “CLOB V2 is live”—support an infrastructure interpretation as much as, if not more than, a token‑launch reading. [PX‑1, PX‑2]
• Exclusions do not resolve the ambiguity. Listing NFTs, promos, and points prevents false positives, but it does not transform every remaining internal ERC‑20 into an “official token launch.” The market’s ordinary reading still requires a token launch as such.

Conclusion
On this record, the plaintiff proved that Polymarket documented a new ERC‑20 collateral token and listed its address, with platform go‑live on April 28, 2026 (04:00 PDT). The plaintiff did not prove that Polymarket launched an official token and publicly communicated that launch. At best, the evidence is in equipoise between “internal collateral upgrade” and “token launch,” which is insufficient under a preponderance standard.

The Council should answer NO to the proposition.

arguments.text:
Council members,

Issue and standard
The proposition asks whether, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket launched an official fungible token and publicly communicated that launch through Polymarket‑controlled channels, excluding NFTs, promos, point systems, and collaborations. Our burden is preponderance: more likely than not.

Bottom line
The record shows Polymarket launched pUSD (Polymarket USD) as a standard ERC‑20 collateral token on Polygon in April 2026 and announced it on Polymarket‑controlled documentation pages, including a dated changelog and migration guide. Polymarket’s Contracts page identifies the unique on‑chain pUSD contract address. This satisfies every element and none of the exclusions. [PX‑1, PX‑2]

Element‑by‑element application
1) Official identification on Polymarket channels
— Polymarket’s own pUSD concept page states that “pUSD (Polymarket USD) is the collateral token used for all trading on Polymarket,” identifies it as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon, and provides functional details (transferability, 6 decimals, USDC‑backed). [PX‑1; summarized in PX‑2]
— The Polymarket Contracts page is described as the “single source of truth” for addresses and lists “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with its production address on Polygon mainnet (chain ID 137). [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— The Polymarket docs changelog and V2 migration guide, both official documentation pages, announce the introduction of a “new collateral token (pUSD)” and then report the production go‑live of the new exchange plus pUSD. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
These are Polymarket‑controlled channels squarely within the definition of “official.”

2) On‑chain availability and unique mapping
— The Contracts page uniquely maps Polymarket’s named token “pUSD” to a single Polygon address (0xC011…2E82DFB) and identifies it as the collateral token proxy. [PX‑2]
— The record includes a Polygonscan capture of that address labeled “Polymarket: pUSD Token,” corroborating that this is a live ERC‑20 with transfers. While explorer labeling is corroborative only, the authoritative linkage is Polymarket’s own Contracts page. [PX‑2]

3) Fungibility and transferability
— pUSD is documented as a standard ERC‑20 on Polygon with 6 decimals. ERC‑20 tokens are transferable and fungible in standard wallets. The record’s concept page and explorer capture show exactly that. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

4) Public communication prior to the deadline (time normalization to PST)
— Changelog entry dated April 17, 2026 announces the upcoming launch of pUSD alongside exchange upgrades with target go‑live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC. April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC equals April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT (UTC‑7). The same day’s entry reports production go‑live and directs users to the migration guide. Both dates are months before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST cutoff. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

5) Exclusions checklist
— Not an NFT: pUSD is an ERC‑20 fungible token. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— Not a promo or points: It is the platform’s collateral token for all trading, not a coupon or non‑transferable credit. [PX‑1]
— Not a partner/community token: The issuer attribution is Polymarket’s own contracts and documentation; “backed by USDC” does not convert issuance into a collaboration launch by another project. [PX‑1; PX‑2]

Addressing anticipated defenses
— “Collateral token ≠ official token.” The proposition requires an “official fungible token” launched and publicly communicated by Polymarket; it does not require governance or revenue‑sharing features. pUSD is an officially introduced, Polymarket‑branded, fungible ERC‑20 deployed for platform use and identified by Polymarket’s own “single source of truth” Contracts page. That squarely fits the ordinary meaning of “official fungible token.” [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— “Announcement vs. launch.” The record shows both: pre‑announcement (Apr 17, 2026) and production go‑live (Apr 28, 2026), with on‑chain address concurrently identified in Polymarket’s Contracts page. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— “Prior disclaimers of any token.” The record contains no contrary Polymarket disclaimer within the period that would negate pUSD. Generic statements in other contexts typically refer to governance/airdrop tokens; even if present, they would not rebut the specific, later official launch of pUSD documented here. (No such contrary exhibit appears in this packet.)

What the record shows vs. our inferences
— Record facts: Polymarket’s pUSD concept page, Contracts page (address mapping), changelog entries with April 2026 dates, V2 migration guide, and a Polygonscan page for the same address. [PX‑1; PX‑2]
— Inference: From “ERC‑20 with 6 decimals on Polygon” and explorer evidence of transfers, we infer fungibility and wallet‑level transferability—consistent with the ERC‑20 standard and the platform role described in the docs.

Disposition
Under a preponderance standard, the better‑aligned, source‑controlled evidence shows Polymarket launched and publicly identified a fungible token—pUSD—well before the January 1, 2027 00:00 PST deadline. The exclusions do not apply. The proposition is therefore more likely than not true. The Council should find for the plaintiff and answer YES.

Exhibits offered
— PX‑1: official-source-record.txt (catalog of captured Polymarket official pages and explorer corroboration)
— PX‑2: primary-evidence.txt (material facts extracted from each captured official page, including the pUSD address and dated changelog entries)
— PX‑3: market-page.txt (verbatim resolution language and exclusions for the question at issue)

Technical report offered
— TR‑1 (PST normalization and token‑classification analysis): Confirms April 28, 2026 11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT and explains why a Polymarket‑documented ERC‑20 collateral token satisfies “official fungible token” under the resolution’s plain language and exclusions.

Council members,

Issue and burden
The proposition requires proof that, before January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST, Polymarket (i) launched a fungible token, (ii) officially released it, and (iii) publicly communicated that launch via Polymarket‑controlled channels. The standard is preponderance: the plaintiff must tip the scales, not merely suggest a possibility.

What the record shows (and what it does not)
— The record’s only evidence comes through plaintiff‑authored summaries PX‑1 and PX‑2. Those summaries quote or paraphrase Polymarket docs pages that describe “a new collateral token (pUSD),” list a Contracts page entry (“pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” with a Polygon address), and include a Changelog with entries dated April 17, 2026 (pre‑announcement) and April 28, 2026 (go‑live ~11:00 UTC = April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT). PX‑2 also cites a Polygonscan page labeled “Polymarket: pUSD Token.”
— Critically, the underlying captured HTML pages are not in the visible record; only after‑the‑fact narrative extracts are. PX‑1 itself concedes the captures were created “after the arbitration,” that server‑dated headers and immutable archives were not preserved, and that timestamps/publication histories remain “worth adding.” No on‑chain block‑time evidence, deployer/owner identity, or cryptographic attribution to Polymarket appears in the exhibits.

Why this does not meet the proposition under preponderance
1) “Launched an official fungible token” vs. internal collateral plumbing. The only Polymarket phrasing the record attributes to official channels is “collateral token (pUSD).” That describes an internal settlement instrument used to operate the exchange, not a public token launch as an independent product. The proposition’s ordinary reading contemplates that Polymarket launched a token as such; the record shows an infrastructure upgrade that happens to implement an ERC‑20 wrapper for USDC. Absent explicit self‑identification as “our official token” or a comparable statement of issuance/launch, the plaintiff’s theory relies on inference, not text.
2) Issuer attribution is unproven. The Contracts page summary is an editable documentation page. The record supplies no immutable archive, no deployer/owner address tying the contract to a Polymarket‑controlled multisig, and no cryptographic link. Polygonscan labeling is expressly “corroborative only” even per PX‑1. On this record, issuance by Polymarket is alleged, not established.
3) Timing is not immutably established on official channels. The dates cited (April 17 and April 28, 2026) appear only as text in a changelog summarized by PX‑1/PX‑2. There are no server headers, archive snapshots, or signed social posts in evidence. Under preponderance, contemporaneous, immutable timestamps matter; the plaintiff chose not to provide them.
4) The structure of the exclusions reinforces the ordinary‑meaning distinction. Excluding NFTs, promos, and point systems signals the market’s focus on a token “launched by Polymarket” in the way that crypto users commonly understand—a token product held/used beyond mere internal accounting. By contrast, the record’s “pUSD — CollateralToken (proxy)” is a USDC‑backed wrapper minted/burned to serve exchange collateralization. Treating any ERC‑20 used internally as an “official token launch” would collapse the resolution’s distinction and sweep in routine contract refactors.

Fallback even under plaintiff’s broader theory
If the Council were inclined to treat any ERC‑20 named in Polymarket docs as an “official token,” the record still fails to uniquely and sufficiently prove: (a) deployer/owner linkage to Polymarket, (b) immutable, pre‑deadline publication of the claimed announcements, and (c) wallet‑level transferability in practice rather than assertion. The plaintiff asks you to bridge each of these with inference.

Record/argument separation
— Record facts: PX‑1/PX‑2 report that Polymarket docs used the words “collateral token (pUSD),” list a contracts address, and show dated changelog entries in April 2026. PX‑1 admits the captures are post‑run preservations and that archival/timestamp evidence is missing.
— Our inferences: From that thin record, it is at least equally plausible that pUSD is an internal wrapper token whose introduction was a technical migration, not an “official token launch” as contemplated by the proposition.

Disposition
On this record, ambiguity persists on the core element—whether Polymarket “launched an official fungible token.” The plaintiff’s evidence depends on editable documentation, lacks immutable timestamps and issuer attribution, and frames a collateral‑contract migration as a token launch. Under a preponderance standard, that does not tip the scales. The Council should find the proposition not proven and answer NO.

Dates for clarity
— Claimed announcements: April 17, 2026; go‑live April 28, 2026 ~11:00 UTC (April 28, 2026 04:00 PDT).
— Deadline: January 1, 2027 at 00:00 PST.

We do not rely on any material outside the visible record; we flag the record’s gaps below for weight and credibility.
